question: Define the interior and the closure of a subset A in a metric space (X, d). Use the concepts of open balls and limit points in your definitions.
expectedPoints: Definition of interior: A point a is an interior point of A if there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(a; ε) is entirely contained in A. The interior of A, denoted int(A), is the set of all interior points of A.

Definition of closure: A point x is a limit point (or adherent point) of A if every open ball B(x; ε) intersects A (i.e., B(x; ε) ∩ A ≠ ∅). The closure of A, denoted cl(A) or Ā, is the union of A and its set of all limit points.
Connection to open balls: Both definitions fundamentally rely on the ability to form open balls of any radius around a point, which is the core concept defining the topology of a metric space.

question: Prove that the interior of a set A is the largest open set contained in A, and the closure of A is the smallest closed set containing A.
expectedPoints: Proof for interior: First, prove int(A) is open by showing for any x in int(A), there is an open ball around x contained in int(A). Then, argue that if U is any open subset contained in A, every point of U is an interior point of A, so U ⊆ int(A). Thus, int(A) is the largest such set.

Proof for closure: First, prove cl(A) is closed by showing its complement is open (if x is not in cl(A), there is an open ball around x that does not intersect A, and hence does not intersect cl(A)). Then, argue that if F is any closed set containing A, then F must contain all limit points of A. Therefore, cl(A) ⊆ F, making it the smallest such set.
Use of definitions: The proof must correctly utilize the definitions of open/closed sets, interior points, and limit points.

question: Consider the set A = { (x, y) ∈ ℝ² : x is rational and y = 0 } with the standard Euclidean metric. Determine the interior, int(A), and the closure, cl(A), of this set. Justify your answers rigorously, explaining why a given point is or is not in the interior or closure.
expectedPoints: Interior is empty: For any point (q, 0) in A and any ε > 0, the open ball B((q,0); ε) will contain points with irrational x-coordinates (and y=0) which are not in A. Therefore, no point in A has an open ball around it entirely within A, so int(A) = ∅.

Closure is the entire x-axis: The x-axis is cl(A) because: 1) A is a subset of the x-axis. 2) The x-axis is closed. 3) Any point (r, 0) on the x-axis (r rational or irrational) is a limit point of A. This is because for any ε > 0, we can find a rational number q in the interval (r-ε, r+ε) (by the density of ℚ in ℝ), making (q, 0) a point of A within ε of (r, 0).
Key concepts applied: Density of rational numbers, construction of sequences converging to any real number, and the definition of limit points using open balls.
